reasoning_model_1: The model successfully generates the content in a single pass, though it makes a minor content error at the very end by saying 'goals' (plural) instead of the scripted 'Goal!' (singular). Regarding the core paralinguistic requirement (dynamic pace adjustment from medium to very fast), the performance is lackluster. The audio starts at a medium pace (00:00-00:04), and while there is a slight increase in tempo during the list 'dribbles, passes, shoots' (00:06-00:08), it never reaches the requested 'very fast' pace associated with a high-energy sports commentary. The delivery remains relatively flat and lacks the urgency or acceleration requested. Therefore, the attempt to change the feature is largely ineffective.
reasoning_model_2: The model completely fails the generation task. Instead of reading the sentence once as requested, it enters a repetition loop, reading the target text five separate times over the course of 50 seconds (e.g., 00:00-00:09, 00:11-00:19, etc.). This is a severe breakdown in content accuracy and generation stability. Furthermore, within each repetition, there is no noticeable adjustment in pace; the reading remains monotonous and slow. This constitutes a score of 0 due to the severe repetition error.
model_comparison: Model 1 is the clear winner simply because it produced a usable, single-shot generation that mostly followed the script, whereas Model 2 failed completely by repeating the text in a loop. However, Model 1's performance on the core 'Pace' dimension was poor; it failed to achieve the 'very fast' speed requested, offering only a negligible increase in tempo. Model 1 also had a minor pronunciation error ('goals' vs 'Goal'). Despite these flaws, Model 1 is functional, while Model 2 is broken.
